sourceText: Continued academic research amidst hardship, wrote seminal works, and established reformist ideas for solving real-world problems

Mencius faced frustration as his attempts to realize his ideal governance in the chaotic Warring States period were met with the reality of political limitations. He also encountered criticism and misunderstanding from those who did not grasp his philosophy.

Despite setbacks, Mencius upheld his convictions. He focused on transmitting his philosophy to future generations by teaching his disciples and authoring the 'Mencius'. Through these efforts, his thought has transcended time and continues to have a profound impact today.

He was subjected to the severe punishment of castration (taizhi) for opposing an imperial decision. This brought not only extreme physical pain but also immense social and mental humiliation. His family and lineage also suffered greatly.

He survived with the sole determination to complete the 'Shiji', overcoming personal disgrace and suffering to pour all his passion into documenting historical truth. His brush became proof of his redemption from humiliation.

He experienced the suffering of a chaotic society and human desires by refusing official positions and living in reclusion. He deeply contemplated the pains caused by societal upheaval and the unbridled pursuit of worldly gains. He struggled to find true freedom within the constraints of reality, distancing himself from conventional values like fame and wealth.

He overcame suffering by pursuing a free spirit that transcended worldly fame and profit. He achieved inner peace by conforming to the natural flow of things, letting go of distinctions, and realizing the fundamental unity of all existence.

He had to build power on the precarious foundation of Shu Han and constantly raise armies amidst the fierce warfare between the three kingdoms. Furthermore, he bore the immense responsibility of assisting a young emperor after Liu Bei's death and faced significant frustration from failed northern expeditions.

He overcame crises with exceptional strategy, meticulous preparation, and unwavering loyalty. Although he did not achieve unification of the realm through his northern expeditions, his efforts allowed Shu Han to survive for several decades.

Initially, Ashoka waged brutal wars for territorial expansion, most notably the Kalinga War, which resulted in immense loss of life and suffering, deeply impacting his psyche and causing profound remorse.
